responseSet.title: Observations of Staff-Support groups with Time-Limited External Facilitation in a Psychiatric Institution
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Observations of Staff-Support groups with Time-Limited External Facilitation in a Psychiatric Institution

Jane Milton and Susan Davison

Susan Davison

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA.

Denmark Hill, London SE5 8AZ

Susan Davison  

It has become customary in many mental health teams to hold regular ‘staff-support groups’. Often the rationale for such groups is unclear, and they are ambivalently regarded. They can be part of a general proliferation of group activities observed in certain institutions, with hazy theoretical underpinning and inadequate leadership and supervision. Frequent requests were being made to the Psychotherapy Unit of a large psychiatric institution for group facilitators to be provided for such staff-support groups. In the past such requests had been responded to on an ad hoc basis, depending on staff availability, and no formal attempt had been made to examine the work done. It was now decided to respond to current requests from two rather disparate Units by providing facilitators on a time-limited basis. There would be an observation both of the ways in which the facilitators were used in the groups and also of how the groups functioned generally. Some relevant literature on institutional dynamics and on staff groups is reviewed. A descriptive account is then given of the functioning of the two facilitated staff groups, with some theoretical discussion of the group dynamics observed. It was striking how differently the groups were used on the two units. Some conclusions are drawn about the need for care in the setting up of such groups, and directions for further research are suggested.

responseSet.title: A Survey of groups within a Psychiatric Hospital
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Survey of groups within a Psychiatric Hospital

Sarah Robertson and Susan Davison

Susan Davison

London SW17 0QT

Denmark Hill, London SE5 8AZ

Susan Davison  

The findings of a survey of the involvement of staff in group activities within a large psychiatric hospital are presented. Over two hundred regular group meetings were being held, excluding ward-rounds and business meetings. There was a wide variation in the type and structure of the groups, the purpose and theoretical underpinning of many of which were unclear to the participants. It is suggested that this lack of clarity reflects the fact that the original therapeutic aims have been subverted in the service of institutionalised defences against anxiety. To be therapeutically effective, groups need clear leadership, good supervision and well-defined aims.

responseSet.title: Emotion and Spirit: Questioning the claims of psychoanalysis and religion. By Neville Symington. London: Cassell. Pp. 197.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emotion and Spirit: Questioning the claims of psychoanalysis and religion. By Neville Symington. London: Cassell. Pp. 197.

Robert Tollemache

Robert Tollemache

Robert Tollemache  

Symington is a perplexing writer. He writes about vital concerns, has good, perceptive things to say, and always illustrates them with accurate, telling case-vignettes; yet often he is diffuse and repetitive; he casually gives words new meanings without careful definitions; and he is unfair and dismissive about other writers.

Here, he is proposing that traditional religion is not relevant to man in the modern world. Psychoanalysis is relevant to man, because it is concerned with emotional action between people living intimately together; but it does not answer questions about purpose, meaning and fulfilment for men and women; hence, religion and psychoanalysis need each other. Many would agree that traditional religion has lost its meaning, and it is a cliché to see psychotherapists as the priest of the new psychoanalytic religion. The hard part is to define the relationship between the two and pinpoint the overlap between them that we sense but do not grasp.

In outline, Symington offers definitions of primitive, mature and natural religions, spirituality and morality, and then reviews Freud's Jung's and other analysts’ approach to religion. He defines narcissism as the most convincing account of human greed, and its transformation as the goal of psychoanalysis, in common with that of all mature religions, he ends by arguing that psychoanalysis's goal of self-knowledge is inseparable from emotional acts of virtue; so it is a spirituality-in-the-world. It undoubtedly is an ambitious attempt to link psychoanalysis and spirituality.

We need not devote much space to his outline of religion, spirituality and morality, as he would be the first to admit that others have written more comprehensively and authoritatively on the subject.

In his review of analytic writing on religion and spirituality, Symington concentrates on Jung's link between individuation and spirituality. Storr (1973) admits ‘It is easy to lose patience with Jung’, and when Symington says ‘There are so many contradictions in Jung that it is difficult to present his views in a coherent way’, it seems he has lost patience, which undermines his approach to Jung's idea of spirituality. This is a sad contrast with his earlier observation (Symington 1986) that ‘We of the Freudian school who have rejected Jung have become impoverished thereby’.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

In this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy we begin with a paper by Gladwell which examines again Freud's famous ‘Dora’ case, looked at here from the angle of sexual abuse. Freud's pioneering piece of work is seen through the eyes of a contemporary clinician who sees Dora as a sexually-abused adolescent. Gladwell takes up issues to do with the reality/fantasy debate, and thinks about the way oedipal themes become distorted in such situations. He highlights particularly the difficulties for male therapists with female patients who have been sexually abused.

The theme of minds and bodies is present in different ways in each of the four remaining papers. Schachter gives us a useful broad overview of the psychoanalytic literature on the mind-body relationship, including some reference to developmental and observational research. Some illustrative clinical examples are given. Skogstad's paper is based on an observational study of a medical ward. He is particularly interested in the ways in which the nursing staff deal with the intense feelings stirred up by daily close contact with patients’ bodies, and with severe illness and death. Skogstad reports on ways in which he sees the ward culture and particular defensive manoeuvres on the part of the staff serving to protect them from becoming overwhelmed by their task.

Our final section, on infant observation in psychoanalytic training, links in to the previous papers in that we are of course looking at a stage of life in which the mind and body are so closely interwoven. The classical mother-infant observation has also become the prototype for other sorts of observations made by clinicians such as Skogstad.

Finally, a letter from Winship continues the important debate about the relationship between nursing and psychotherapy.

[[RunningHead]]

Affect Regulation and the Origin of the Self: The Neurobiology of Emotional Development. By Allan N Schore. New Jersey: Lawrence Erlbaum $99.95. Pp. 670.

Patricia Hughes

Patricia Hughes

Patricia Hughes  

Cartesian Dualists stand and be counted! Just what is the relationship between mind and brain? This is certainly not a question which cropped up during my training in psychoanalysis, and I suspect that while psychoanalysts accept that there is indeed a connection, many of us have had rather little curiosity about what that connection might be. We are impressively broad-minded in allowing two schools of psychoanalytic thought to coexist in the Institute of Psycho-Analysis and training, and have no need to expand our horizons further.

Science used to be the domain of the specialist and the academic, and not an area that the person on the Clapham omnibus would know or have an opinion about. But society's relationship with science is changing fast. Popular science is available to all, and many interested people read and derive their views from the national press. It is depressing to read ill-informed attacks on Freud, sometimes from respected scientists; but, on the whole, psychoanalysts have done little to raise the quality of information from stream of criticism to informed discussion. Perhaps an important step in doing this will be to educate ourselves about where we stand in relation to the other sciences which explore the mind, and to learn the language of those sciences which are relevant to, but different from, psychoanalysis.

Our discipline is not the same kind of science as the physical sciences of chemistry and physics. We cannot make a hypothesis which can be empirically tested, and make general laws which will have secure predictability. Yet we know that in the ‘human’ sciences which study social behaviour (Sociology, Anthropology, Law), it is recognised that some knowledge cannot rely on verifiability in the same way as in the physical sciences, and attacks on psychoanalysis for not being ‘scientific’ are unjustified.

There is a curious anomaly in how many analysts think about the mind. We work with the human mind and recognise the central importance of its development, but we may choose to ignore the brain and its parallel development. If mind and brain are inextricably related then there must be some instances in which the activities of the physical sciences are relevant to what we do. And if we remain ignorant of well-validated evidence of how the brain works in constructing our models of the mind, then we may deserve the criticism we so easily attract. In the psychoanalytic method we have a unique technique for exploring the human mind in all its subtlety and individuality, but we must take account of other ways of investigating and of knowledge which derives from other sources. Our theories of the mind in our non-physical science must be compatible with what is known from the physical sciences.

responseSet.title: Working in a World of Bodies Defensive Techniques on a Medical ward a Psychoanalytical Observation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Working in a World of Bodies Defensive Techniques on a Medical ward a Psychoanalytical Observation

Wilhelm Skogstad

Wilhelm Skogstad

1 Ham Common, Richmond, Surrey, TW10 7JF.

Wilhelm Skogstad  

This paper describes the observation of a medical ward carried out in a general hospital in weekly visits over a period of four months, and attempts a psychoanalytic understanding of the observations. Nurses working with physically ill patients face deep anxieties and conflicts, particularly fear of death and illness, of being overwhelmed by their feelings of pity, guilt and resentment; but also fear of the sexual impulses that close physical contact with patients stirs up. The paper shows that these anxieties were dealt with by a number of defensive techniques. Although the nurses sometimes showed the ability to be sensitive, the general culture was very defensive. The various defensive techniques commonly used are described and illustrated. Amongst them were: manic excitement and erotisation; a constant moving of patients with little recognition of their feelings about that; a strong use of the body, so that patients were often not related to as whole persons; and defence mechanisms of a particularly concrete kind.

The Therapeutic Relationship in Systemic Therapy. Edited by Carmel Flaskas & Amaryll Perlesz London: Karnac. Pp. 225.

Paula Boston

Paula Boston

Paula Boston  

The Therapeutic Relationship in Systemic Therapy is one of a series of publications about systemic family therapy by the British-based editors David Campbell and Ros Draper. The editors, and most contributors to this particular book, are Australian; and there is a commentary by two prominent British family therapists. The book is organised into two sections: theory and application. Its structure is representative of the interesting and rich collaborations between these two regions of the world. The strength of mutual influence is obvious: from the inclusion of comments by psychoanalytic migrants from Britain, like Neville Symington; and the impact of Australian Michael White on family therapy in Britain.

The central thesis of the work, the relationship aspect of systemic therapy, is timely. A major theme amongst trainers in family therapy is the development of the therapist in terms of what they ‘bring with them and are involved in creating’ when they are with families.

[[RunningHead]]

Prioritizing among Patients Seeking Subsidized Psychotherapy

Rolf Sandell and Gunilla Fredelius

Gunilla Fredelius

Björngårdsgatan 25, S-118 52 Stockholm.

Gunilla Fredelius  

Prioritizing patients for subsidized psychotherapy is necessary when resources are scarce. In this study, three groups of ten judges each, representing the professional, clinical perspective, the political decision-making perspective, and the layman's perspective, respectively, were given the task of deciding which of fifteen patients on a waiting-list for psychotherapy and psychoanalysis they would recommend for subsidization. Statistical analyses showed similarities between the priorities of laymen and decision-makers, whereas clinicians prioritized the patients differently. The regression weights of patient variables in explaining the groups’ priorities suggest that laymen and decision-makers tend to focus on the urgency of a case from a humanitarian point of view, whereas clinicians try to balance such considerations against the apparent suitability of the case for psychotherapeutic treatment.

responseSet.title: Use and Abuse in the Countertransference
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Use and Abuse in the Countertransference

Ayesha Khwaja

Ayesha Khwaja

London, NW2 6HG.

Ayesha Khwaja  

Historically the countertransference, like the transference, was initially seen as a hindrance which came in the way of the analyst having a clear and objective view of his patient. Seen as a resistance on the part of the analyst, a result of his own neurotic conflicts, the countertransference was considered to be something which should be worked through in his own analysis. Over time, this has changed and the countertransference is now seen as an important part of the analytic relationship. In this paper I want to focus on my countertransference in the work with a particular patient, to look at the changes in it over the course of the therapy, and in my ability to be more consciously aware of it. I also want to look at the links between projective identification and countertransference, and in particular at the use of the former to control the object and to avoid separateness. The struggle to face reality is not just a problem for the patient, but to some extent also for the therapist, and I want to explore the resistance in both parties.

Errica Moustaki-Smilansky  

What makes for mature relating, such as in the ‘capacity for intimacy’ which allows for the partners’ separateness but nonetheless enables deep closeness? How can this capacity be meaningfully conceptualised? How can appearances of intimacy be unravelled as being no more than a mirage covering up two people imprisoned in a ‘gridlock’ of intense denial of difference and separateness? What are the role and use of projective identification in choice of partner and in the couple's relationship?

These are some core questions which are addressed in this interesting book which includes, for the most part, revised versions of the contributors’ papers presented in a Tavistock Marital Studies Institute conference held in November 1994, titled ‘Projective Identification and the couple: Intrusion? Communication?’

Psychoanalytic Observation in Community and Primary Health Care Education

Meira Likierman

Meira Likierman

5 Downshire Hill, LONDON, NW3 1NR

Meira Likierman  

This paper describes a psychoanalytic observation seminar that was set up as part of an MA in Community and Primary Health Care programme. The seminar was intended for senior professionals outside psychotherapy trainings, drawn from the health and social-care fields. The author outlines the way in which a conventional infant-observation model was adapted for this group, enabling the students to observe both individual subjects and also diverse primary-health and community-care settings. The advantages of such a learning experience are discussed; and also the inevitable anxieties stirred by it. Several examples of observations are provided, showing how students were helped to negotiate and explore challenging primary-health and community-care settings.

responseSet.title: The Sound Link: Psychodynamic Group Music Therapy in a Therapeutic Community Setting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sound Link: Psychodynamic Group Music Therapy in a Therapeutic Community Setting

David Stewart BMus, DipMT, RMTh

David Stewart

5 Upper Malone Crescent, Belfast.

David Stewart  

This paper explores the efficacy of on-going weekly psychodynamic group music therapy with those with chronic mental illness in a therapeutic-community setting. Co-improvised music-making was the primary medium of communication and connection in the group, combined with a period for verbal reflection. Central to the therapist's thesis is that the shared music medium primarily acts as a way of expressing, organising and containing the experience of individual members and of the group. It allows for contact between members at a pre-verbal, preconscious level. For this particular patient group, contacts made at this level can be critical to their further ability to tolerate and integrate feelings in a more verbal and conscious way. The music-therapy setting is seen as aiding the process of linking together these two areas of experience, thus providing patients with a potentially freer, less restricted, way of relating to themselves and each other.

responseSet.title: Mother-Baby Observation: The Position of the Observer
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mother-Baby Observation: The Position of the Observer11

Penelope Crick

Penelope Crick

2e Queen Elizabeth's Walk, London N16 0HX.

Penelope Crick  

In this paper, I will suggest that the particular potency of mother-baby observation as part of psychoanalytic training lies not so much in what is learned about babies, as in the work that has to go on in the analytic candidate to find internally ‘the position of the observer’. This is an essential part of what is involved in eventually acquiring a psychoanalytic identity. In presenting some material from the observation that I made while I was training, I want to draw attention to the matter of ‘finding a position from which to make observations’. It is here, I think, that continuity between infant-observation and psychoanalytic work is found. In both, finding a position is one thing, but sustaining it in dynamic and pressurising circumstances is quite another.

responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

Jane Milton  

In this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy the first three papers look at work in three different NHS settings.

Bell's account of work in an in-patient therapeutic community looks at critical issues in such work. This includes in particular how tension between, on the one hand individual psychotherapy, and on the other, nursing and community treatment, arises; and how the author believes that this needs to be managed as far as possible with the maintenance of a classical psychotherapeutic setting.

Morton's paper from an outpatient clinic describes individual psychoanalytic psychotherapy with a woman who felt ‘haunted’ by the ghost of her father, and draws on some of the scarce psychological literature on ghosts in thinking about the psychic phenomena involved.

Stewart's paper departs somewhat from our usual range, in describing a music-therapy group in a residential community setting for people with severe chronic mental illness. Stewart argues that music therapy can be conceptualised and applied using a psychoanalytical perspective. After a detailed literature review, he illustrates his point with clinical material showing the way in which he understands the use of transference and countertransference with this non-verbal medium.

The following three papers are on the subject of assessment for psychoanalytic psychotherapy. The first two (by Milton and Schachter) originate from a recent APP conference on the subject, and address the complex issues around suitability for treatment. My paper takes a somewhat macroscopic overview of a subject which Schachter then tackles more microscopically through detailed clinical material, with ideas held in common as well as some divergences. The final paper, by Sandell & Fredelius, offers an unusual perspective on the subject, describing research in which professional and lay panels rated patients’ priority for psychotherapy, on the basis of clinical vignettes. Interesting differences in judgment emerged between the groups.

responseSet.title: The Story of a Ghost
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Story of a Ghost

Mary Morton

Mary Morton

Thurmaston Lane, Leicester, LE5 0TA.

Mary Morton  

This paper considers the nature of ghosts from an analytical perspective, drawing from clinical work and also from Fairbairn's ideas on internalisation. Ghosts can be seen as a manifestation of the return of the dissociated parts of the self. Their origins lie in the response to early traumatic experience where the separateness of self and object is denied. Ghosts can be laid to rest through a process of separation, re-integration and re-attribution, a process which reflects a move from the paranoid-schizoid position to the depressive position.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Why Freud was Wrong: Sin, Science, and Psychoanalysis. By Richard Webster. London: Harper Collins. £25.00 hb; £9.99 pb. Pp. 673.

Philip Crockatt

Philip Crockatt

Philip Crockatt  

The crude, main title of this very long book suggests a critique of Freudian ideas; and indeed the first thirteen chapters, some 250 pages, comprising Part I, entitled ‘The Creation of a Pseudo-Science’, lend weight to this view of Webster's intentions as purely destructive. With great relish, he sets about bashing (there is no other word for it) Freud the man, the theorist and the therapist, recounting the by-now familiar critiques of such writers as Grunbaum, Gioffi, Crews, plus many more, and adding what he claims to be new exposures of the fallacies of Freud's work. None of his ideas is new. They are in the main a re-telling of old stories about the allegedly false premises upon which psychoanalysis is based. There is, for example, according to Webster, quoting the work of Slater, published in the 1950s, and Thornton, whose book came out twenty years ago, no such thing as hysteria. Freud got it wrong, as did Charcot and the whole of nineteenth- and twentieth-century psychiatry, by misdiagnosing cases of mild temporal-lobe epilepsy as a psychological illness. This is an old chestnut, as Webster finally admits, after many pages on the subject.

The real problem with the concept of hysteria is that it can, and has been, used as a diagnostic dustbin; as have many other diagnoses, schizophrenia for example, to conceal uncertainty. Webster does not actually argue with the phenomenon of hysterical symptoms as classically described. He discusses in a similar, ambivalent vein other central Freudian discoveries, such as the unconscious mind, the theory of childhood sexuality, as well as evidence about the efficacy of psychoanalysis, and Freud's claim to be a scientist. In all of these lengthy outpourings Webster does not in my view add anything to existing critiques of psychoanalysis. Nor does he appear ever to have discussed his ideas with a modern psychoanalyst, or read many psychoanalytic books or journals. Like many recent critics of psychoanalysis he appears to be ignorant of developments since Freud, and therefore of the radical changes in basic theory and practice that have taken place in the last hundred years. He has thereby robbed himself of the discovery that most, if not all, of the points he raises have been discussed at length in the analytic literature.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Psychoanalysis: Contemporary Theory and Practice. By Anthony Bateman, & Jeremy Holmes. Routledge £40.00 (hb), £12.99 (pb). Pp. 290.

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake

Nollaig Drake  

The stated aim of this book is to

…attempt to bring together the varying strands of psychoanalytic theory and practice to highlight their common ground and differences.

Anthony Bateman is a Consultant Psychotherapist and a Member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society, while Jeremy Holmes is a Consultant Psychiatrist and

178

Psychotherapist. The authors bring their complementary clinical trainings and considerable combined clinical experience to bear on the development of this interesting collaborative project in which they hope to clarify, question and extract what is valuable from each psychoanalytic viewpoint and to bring us up-to-date on contemporary psychoanalytic thought. The book is divided into two parts, the first being devoted to theory while the second is concerned with practice. The volume is completed with an extensive reference section.

At the outset, we are introduced to some of the key issues of debate and controversy which are alive in contemporary psychoanalysis. As part of the necessary context within which these need to be considered, we are offered a simplified account of the history and development of the psychoanalytic movement, through some of its leading exponents and across continental and cultural divides. Among the contentious issues with which contemporary psychoanalysis continues to struggle, Bateman & Holmes start with the definition of psychoanalysis itself; and while outlining the main viewpoints currently under debate, they arrive at their own working definition, and emphasise that theirs is a provisional view which may well change over time, depending on further clinical experience and theoretical developments. Psychoanalytic therapy is seen by them

…as a spectrum from ‘full’ psychoanalysis to psychoanalytic psychotherapy, from the use of methods that are expressing to those that are supportive, from interpretation to ‘holding’.

Thus, right from the beginning, our attention is engaged as the authors convey the vitality and diversity of contemporary psychoanalysis.

The Broad Spectrum Psychotherapist. By Wyn Bramley. Free Association Books. £15.95. Pp. 219The Supervisory Couple in Broad Spectrum Psychotherapy. Also by Wyn Bramley. Free Association Books. £16.95. Pp. 195.

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

Wyn Bramley came from a background of psychosocial nursing at the Cassel Hospital to train as a group analyst during the 1970s. She now practises and supervises psychodynamic psychotherapy, both privately and within various NHS settings in Oxford. These two books were published last year, and would seem, between them, to offer an account of her own style of working with patients and with supervisees. The emphasis throughout is on work with individuals: group and couple work is not directly covered.

There is much to applaud in these books, which demonstrate a humane, professional and considered approach to the many kinds of patients and therapy situations which may present to the Broad Spectrum Psychotherapist. This therapist is characterised as one who is working in settings like GP surgeries, hospitals or day-centres, church or voluntary services, or in private practice. Such a therapist is offering a psychodynamic approach in short- or medium-term work, but may have little control over the kinds of patients she is asked to treat, and may often be functioning as what Bramley likes to call a ‘GP to the Mind’. This work is contrasted, perhaps somewhat defensively, with the more specialised long-term work of the analyst in private practice.

The Emotional Needs of Young Children and Their Families: Using Psychoanalytic Ideas in the Community. Edited by Judith Trowell & Marion Bower. Routledge £14.99 (pb). Pp. 299.

Patricia Robertson

Patricia Robertson

Patricia Robertson  

In the description of the book, the editors state that no previous knowledge of the subject, that is psychodynamic ideas, is assumed, and they set the scene for this by three introductory chapters. They continue this setting process by linked chapters, in order to put the subsequent chapters into perspective in relation to psychoanalytic ideas and their social or institutional settings. The settings include: the Health Service, premature-baby units, GP services, and health-visitor services; in Social Services, guardian-ad-litern work, residential work and day-care; in Education settings, work with children including small-group work with young children who have experienced fragmented care, and work to support refugee children in schools.

From a description like this, it can be seen that the book runs the risk of being a collection of accounts of psychoanalytically-informed work without being a cohesive whole.

For the psychoanalytically-informed reader this is perhaps less important. Such a reader can dip in at will if looking to find out what is possible and what has been done elsewhere, and to find useful references about such work in a particular setting. The acid test of whether the book works is in relation to the psychoanalytically-uninformed reader. I think it is likely to meet this test — the clinical material is described in sufficient detail to carry the reader along. A good example of this is Savi McKenzie Smith's chapter on Cross-Cultural Issues and Racism in a Multicultural Society, McKenzie Smith devotes enough attention to the path of the therapy to enable someone unfamiliar with this kind of work to follow it.

184

[[RunningHead]]

Forensic Psychotherapy. Crime, Psychodynamics and the Offender Patient. Edited by Christopher Cordess & Murray Cox. Two volumes. Jessica Kingsley. £165. Pp. 866.

Robert Hale

Robert Hale

Robert Hale  

This is a fascinating book — as much in the conception as in the execution. As a reviewer, I did what few will do: I read through all 866 pages by sixty-six authors. Add on the 150 pages of index and references (approximately 1400) and you must have one of the longest books to be published in psychotherapy. Obviously it is a ‘curate's egg’ - bad in parts - but it is remarkable how enjoyable and interesting so much of it is. My guess is that the authors invited all the people they know to be professionally involved in the emerging sub-specialty of forensic psychotherapy to write on a subject largely of their own choice. The result is an enormously varied collection of papers, ranging in length from three to thirty-six pages. The book is divided into two volumes, Mainly Theory and Mainly Practice. The first is more coherent; the second more a potpourri. Inevitably there is much duplication of themes. Fierce editing might have removed some of this, but would have destroyed many of the papers which were really written to be read on their own.

The emphasis is on psychoanalytic theory and its application in the individual and group setting. Other forms of therapy - cognitive behaviour, systems theory, drama therapy, art therapy, music therapy, dance therapy — are all given a chapter. Perhaps this emphasis on the psychodynamic reflects the current bias within the Health Service, but it does not reflect the position in the Prison Service, of which the 57,000 users must represent the largest collection of potential beneficiaries for forensic psychotherapy in the country. Here the dominant theory is cognitive-behavioural.

185

responseSet.title: Letter to the Editor
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Gary Winship

Gary Winship

Gary Winship  

Dear Dr Milton

David Bell's (1997) paper in the last edition of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy about in-patient psychotherapy at the Cassel is a further argument in favour of a demarcation between the roles of nurse and therapist in fostering the mutative processes in the treatment of patients in the psychotherapeutic treatment milieu. Bell's views concur with those expressed by Bob Hinshelwood and Sue Robinson (1996) in a letter to the journal last year in response to my review of the historical developments in psychoanalytically-oriented nursing (Winship 1996). The triangular relationship between the nurse, therapist and patient in the Cassel therapeutic community, was well described previously by James (1984), and Bell's paper suggests that many of the same treatment contingencies are pertinent today.

The argument in favour of making a clear distinction between the role of nurse and that of psychotherapist is born out of a concern that nurses will cease to do nursing if they do psychotherapy. However, it is difficult to describe the work of some clinical nurse-specialists who work in psychotherapy services as anything other than psychotherapy. This dilemma has been one of the main talking-points at the first two APP nursing conferences, and was also discussed at the Institute of Psychoanalysis when a special evening was assigned to nursing. Contrary to Bob Hinshelwood and Sue Robinson's position on this, there is a great deal of impetus for less role-distinction. My feeling is that it is important to identify what is unique about the contribution of psychiatric nursing in the delivery of psychoanalytic psychotherapy in the public sector; at the moment those nurses with training as psychotherapists who are already working in the field of NHS psychotherapy have roles which are less easily identified. This is, I hope, a temporary confusional state, but it is one that is helpful insofar as it means that psychotherapy is being seen as less out-of-bounds for some nurses who are now beginning to argue for ownership (Macmahon 1994). Since the mid-1980s, the Nurses Association of Psychodynamic Psychotherapists (NAPP) has been promoting the work of forty-to-fifty full members who are working in public-sector psychotherapy departments. NAPP members have been exploring alternatives to the notion that formal psychotherapy is always carried out by non-nurses and is seeking to clarify the position of a specialist register of nurse-psychotherapists with the UKCC. It would seem timely for the APP to participate in this discussion and to influence the setting of approved standards.

responseSet.title: Heinz Kohut and the Psychology of the Self. By Allen M Siegel. Routledge Pp. 226.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Heinz Kohut and the Psychology of the Self. By Allen M Siegel. Routledge Pp. 226.

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines

Malcolm Pines  

Heinz Kohut's writings form the core of the psychoanalytic movement known as Self Psychology. Allen Siegel acknowledges that Kohut is difficult to read: his style

276

is Germanic, so one searches his lengthy sentences for the verb that will bring sense and order in contrast to his renown as a fluent coherent extempore speaker. Siegel has rendered Kohut's writing into an attractive readable text. There are several informative clear diagrams which outline both Freud's and Kohut's theories of psychic structure.

In the opening chapter, Ernest Wolf, Kohut's close collaborator, sketches Kohut's personality:

A strange mixture of aloof, aristocratic and almost puritanical austerity in a warmly responsive and considerate person; a very private person.

A curious mixture indeed! Kohut was intensely musical, a wine buff, an ardent runner before running became widespread. He concealed for ten years the chronic leukaemia from which he died, and gave his final address only three days before his death.

Kohut was a Vice-President of the American Psychoanalytical Association. Anna Freud stayed with him when she visited Chicago. It is important to recognise his stature in that he was an influential teacher at the Chicago Institute of Psychoanalysis before he developed his own theories. Siegel gives a good account of his admired two-year course on psychoanalysis given between 1958 and the late ‘6s, in which he stressed the continuous change in psychoanalytic theory. At that time it was in contrast to the dogmatic teaching that characterised many of the other North-American institutes. His 1963 paper, written jointly with Philip Seitz, reprinted in The Search for the Self is a model on the concepts and theories of psychoanalysis.

Siegel admires Kohut, and believes that his contribution, like Freud's, will prove of enduring value. He states that Kohut created a new atmosphere in the consulting-room. Convinced that the defective Self is responsible for the patient's sufferings, the analyst listens to the Self and hears its struggle to survive. Kohut's shift from a psychology of hidden drives to a psychology of the driven Self changes the therapeutic encounter from a subtly adversarial experience to a friendly partnership. Empathy was his guide and prolonged empathic immersion is the primary analytic tool. Theory evolves from this encounter; ‘experience near’ is privileged over ‘experience distance.

[[RunningHead]]

Rooted Sorrows. Psychoanalytic Perspectives on Child Protection, Assessment, Therapy and Treatment. Edited by the Hon. Mr Justice Wall. Jordan. Family Law series. Pp. 152 (A4 format).

Cleo Van Velsen

Cleo Van Velsen

Cleo Van Velsen  

This concise and interesting volume should be essential reading for all clinicians and advocates involved in the complex area of care orders, family dysfunction, and assessment of parenting. It is the result of a multidisciplinary conference, held in September 1985, which included judges, psychiatrists and other professions within the family justice system, involved in the protection and care of children. Papers were distributed in advance, to facilitate discussion, and the number of participants was kept small. This book reproduces the papers, responses and ensuing discussions, as well as having a brief commentary.

Mr Justice Thorpe (at the time a judge in the Family Court and currently a Lord Justice of Appeal) highlights the concern of many judges to understand more about theories of development of mind, and he himself finds psychoanalytic perspective of great value. However, he highlights the problems of mutual incomprehension between clinicians and lawyers, particularly in learning about a process which ‘seems individualistic if not competitive’ (p. 3) with language which is ‘highly technical and apparently exclusive of those who have not an understanding of the fundamental concepts’ (p. 4). The few exceptions he quotes are Anna Freud, Winnicott and Bowlby. Justice Thorpe's contribution reminds us all that reports which convey psychodynamic understanding should do so clearly. He implies that we clinicians should read more in other disciplines about how we can be experienced.

Mr Justice Wall (a judge of the Family Division of the High Court) contributes a complex paper investigating the use of expert witnesses. It clarifies the definition of an expert, but also the implications of such evidence. There is a masterly teasing out of the role of expert vis-à-vis the judge, concluding with his views that it is the ‘expert who advises and the judge who decides’ (p. 37). This is of course no easy matter in the Family Court, when the child in question very rarely gives direct evidence, and thus the court has to rely on reports. Included also is a discussion of whether an expert can comment on the credibility of a child, or an adult, and how this may interfere with the judge's task of deciding fact. Such an analysis is vital for expert witnesses in any branch of the criminal justice system.

Grief in Children: A Handbook for Adults. By Atle Dyregrov. London: Jessica KingsleyBeing and Not Being: Clinical Applications of the Death Instinct. By Otto Weininger. Connecticut: International Universities Press.

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell  

Children and Death are powerful subjects. There are the powerful images of accidents and atrocities — Dunblane, Bosnia, Rwanda — but also the distress of the ‘ordinary’ bereavements — the child whose parent dies from illness or an accident, the children whose classmate dies, the children whose sibling dies. How do we as adults and professionals do what we can to help in these situations? Both these books are very clear that unless we have individually come to terms with death, our own mortality, and the past or future deaths of our parents and our children, we cannot be of much help at all.

Atle Dyregrov is a Norwegian psychologist who has worked with bereaved and dying children; and this is a useful small book which could be helpful to parents, relatives, teachers and primary-care workers. The book is full of practical suggestions and urges that children be talked to in a straightforward, direct way. Dyregrov shows how euphemisms can be confusing and damaging: the finality and permanence of death should not be avoided by ideas such as sleep, a long journey, the after-life. He is very keen not to pathologise the bereaved or dying, and to emphasise that death is a part of the life-cycle, even when it is untimely death. It is society that wants to avoid recognition of death, because it feels unbearable to have to face it.

[[RunningHead]]

Science, Systems and Psychoanalysis and Clinical Practice and the Architecture of the Mind. Two books by Robert Langs. London: Karnac.

David Morgan

David Morgan

David Morgan  

I knew from previous clinical experience something about Langs's emphasis, some might say over-emphasis, of the uses and abuses of the setting and the importance of the setting as a frame. It is true that Langs made an extremely important contribution to the study of the setting, and deviations from it, and the effects of these on the patient. He emphasises that what we might take for granted as therapists about ourselves and the frame within which we work, recreates the earliest experiences and dependencies, and that many of the patient's responses to us are in fact triggered by perceived changes or deviations from this setting. It is the therapist's job to listen to the patient, in particular his communications to us about our perceived or real failings. He postulates that most of the patient's communications are in fact coded responses, not only about their unconscious but about their deep perception of the therapist, and about alterations to the setting which resonate with previous experiences. These triggers, and the consequent adaptation to them in the patient, have to be decoded by the sensitive analyst aware that his own behaviour is being communicated back to him via the patient's coded response. It is working through these triggers and the patients’ adaptive responses that constitutes the real work of therapy, according to Langs. He has developed a whole theory around this postulate which is in part convincing. Having said this, I do feel that when one has read a book by Langs, and understood his basic call for a more sensitive approach to the patient's deep transference to the analyst, it is unnecessary to read any more.

As Bion has described, all truths and apparent understanding can be used to obscure deeper truths. Like many theorists, Langs seems to want to build a whole framework and theory about his notion. This tendency of course is not limited to Langs. Whatever theory we hold is limited. To assume that all the patient's responses are always responses to the therapist and the deviations in the therapeutic frame, seems to deny the patient's own reality. I contrast this with Betty Joseph's ‘Transference: the Total Situation’, in which she gives due emphasis to the here-and-now reactions to the analyst and the setting; but does it in a way that does not create the impression that all a patient's responses are triggers to the analyst's behaviour, utterances or deviations from the frame; but a complex interaction that is a combination of many interrelated factors. Joseph regards the patient's acting-out in the session as our richest source of information about his internal world, his object-relations and his history. This seems to suggest that the feelings and thoughts aroused in the analyst in the countertransference are not just communications to the analyst about the analyst, but the deepest communications about the patient's inner world.

responseSet.title: Marion Burgner
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Marion Burgner

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy  

Marion Burgner, who died on 1 October 1996, was one of the founder members of the editorial board of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy. Her contributions to public-sector work were many and varied, and included clinical work and research into adolescent breakdown at the Brent Consultation Centre, teaching for many years at University College Hospital, and working with an HIV/Aids research group at the Tavistock and Portman Clinics. Her important intellectual contributions are described below.

Marion Burgner's Intellectual Contribution

The core theme of Marion Burgner's contribution was development. She attended carefully to the lessons of Anna Freud, and in several papers, in a period spanning two-and-a-half decades, she worked on integrating the developmental perspective with other emerging ideas from the Hampstead Clinic, including the notions of the representational world and ideas emerging from the attempts at reconciling classical theory with clinical experience with severely disturbed children, adolescents and young adults.

In a prescient paper presented in 1979 with Hansi Kennedy (Burgner & Kennedy 1980), she addressed the problem of sado-masochistic behaviour in children. She pointed out the risk of confusing the aggression of the child in the anal phase with sadism. Anal sadism is a misnomer, since the intention to inflict pain and to derive pleasure from such an action, as we now know, is the achievement of a subsequent phase. She appropriately linked sadism to the phallic-oedipal configuration and the narcissistic pattern of relating characteristic of this period where gratification is sought via omnipotence, control, domination and denigration of the object, safeguarding the integrity of the self-representation. The vulnerability of the self originated in the cruelty of early parent-child relations where physical pain comes to be a predominant part of relating to an unloving caregiver. The masochistic stance was seen as a part of the seduction of the aggressor by which the child seeks to change an unloving attitude in the caregiver to a loving one. The masochistic patient's attachment is to pain, commonly because of the mother's early, intrusive, painful but sexually exciting interactions with her child. Thus the disturbed mother-child relationship was seen as subsequently colouring later sado-masochistic manifestations.

responseSet.title: Mother-Baby Observation: Introductory Remarks to the Two Papers by the Authors
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mother-Baby Observation: Introductory Remarks to the Two Papers by the Authors

Penelope Crick and Erika Bard

Erika Bard

Erika Bard  

Versions of our papers that appear in this issue of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy were prepared for a session commemorating D W Winnicott's centenary at the British Psycho-Analytical Society's 1996 English Speaking Conference. In recent years the term ‘infant observation’ to describe what has become a conventional part of psychoanalytical and many psychotherapy trainings tends to have become renamed ‘mother-baby observation’ to acknowledge the impossibility of separating the observed infant from the mother. But the basic task has remained the same since its introduction as part of child-psychotherapy training in the 1940s: a student visits the home of a new baby and its family once-weekly for about an hour for the first year, or in some trainings two years, of the infant's life. The student tries to attain an attitude of benign but detached interest in the baby and its mother (and of course other family members) and has the task of writing up the observed hour for discussion at a weekly seminar led by an experienced member of the relevant training organisation.

In general, these observational exercises are a preliminary to commencing clinical work with patients, and it is almost becoming de rigueur for any training that aspires to have psychoanalytic credibility. But in what way does the mother-baby observation prepare the student for their later clinical work? What is the observation for? What is its function in a training? Is it to train the student in some capacities, or in order to assess a student's capacities? It is not our intention here to attempt to come to any conclusion about the various ways in which infant-observation is seen to contribute to a psychoanalytic training — there is a wide literature, all of which makes interesting and varying points, and none of which gives definitive agreements and conclusions. (See Some Further Reading p. 268-9 below.)

Nonetheless, there are several general principles with which it would be impossible to disagree. For example, that it is important for psychoanalysts and psychoanalytic psychotherapists to know something of infantile development; that to be able to observe quietly without being inappropriately overwhelmed with excitement or anxiety is a valuable quality in a clinician; and that it is also important to be able to make observations of emotionally meaningful phenomena that affect one as the observer without using theoretical frameworks in a self-defensive way that may obscure significant features of the material. Of course, to assume that one approaches mother-baby observation without any theoretical influence is naive and simplistic; one needs to be alert to how a favourite theory may creep in and affect what one ‘sees’.

Inpatient Psychotherapy: The Art of the Impossible11

David Bell

David Bell

Flat 4, Mullion Court Finchley Road, London NW3 5JH.

David Bell  

This paper discusses some critical issues that arise in the psychotherapeutic treatment of patients in an in-patient setting. The paper describes aspects of the work of the Adult Unit at the Cassel Hospital, and the difficulties that arise from the less-private nature of the psychotherapeutic treatment in that setting. The author particularly focuses on the relation between the individual psychotherapy on the one hand, and the nursing and community treatment on the other, and argues for the need for clear demarcation of roles between these different functions. This imposes particular stress on the psychotherapist and nursing staff. Examples are given to illustrate this. The author emphasises the importance of maintaining as far as possible a classical psychotherapeutic setting, whilst recognising the necessity for communication between the psychotherapist and the other members of the therapeutic team.

The author also describes the ways in which certain individual psychopathologies can resonate within the institutional structure, causing serious deterioration in the therapeutic work. He particularly focuses on the ways in which the ‘psychotic superego’ can find a home within institutional life.

Why Assess? Psychoanalytical Assessment in the NHS11

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA.

Jane Milton  

The author outlines an approach to assessment, or as she prefers to call it, psychoanalytic consultation, in the Health Service. This involves giving the patient an experience of the analytic process, which they can then assess in a way, while the assessor gains information about a number of categories that can be thought of hierarchically, which inform the advice given the patient and the referrer about management and treatment. The safety of the patient is paramount, as psychoanalytic psychotherapy is a powerful and disruptive treatment. However it is argued that, on the whole, ‘suitability for psychotherapy’ should be on the basis of exclusion rather than involving the patient passing a sort of ‘suitability test’. The latter may enable public psychotherapy services to deny the incompleteness of what they are able to offer.

The Story of a Mental Hospital: Fulbourn 1858-1982. By David H. Clark. London: Process Press. Pp. 254. £19.95.

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

This likable book on the first 125 years of Fulbourn Hospital, Cambridge, is partly the history of a typical English county asylum. It is also an autobiographical account by the last Medical Superintendent of the pioneering work he did there from 1952 until 1983. Many, though perhaps not all, of those who know David Clark and worked with him at Fulbourn Hospital will be delighted to see his book. They will search the excellent index, and riffle through the pages, looking for famous stories and characters. They will not be disappointed.

In an interesting Foreword, Roy Porter points out how surprisingly rare it has been for the psychiatrists involved in running such hospitals in this century to write about their experiences. By contrast, in the nineteenth century, ‘their Victorian precursors seem to have been vocal on the subject to the point of garrulity’. In his first two chapters, Clark plunders to good effect such descriptions, as well as local newspaper reports and the yearly comments of the Lunacy Commissioners, as he tells the story of Fulbourn Hospital's first hundred years.

The Lunacy Act of 1845 required each county to provide accommodation for pauper lunatics, and so in 1858 the building was opened, some three miles south-west of Cambridge.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Theory of mind in the Borderline Patient and its Relation to the Question of Lying

Ilan Diamant

Ilan Diamant

Gower Street, London WC1 6BT.

Ilan Diamant  

The paper makes a clear distinction between mature and immature lying. Normal lying is defined as a mechanism aiming to manipulate another's mind; its direction is to change another's belief, and thus another's behaviour. The paper introduces a cognitive-developmental ‘Theory of Mind’, and suggests that this a fundamental prerequisite for lying. This means the liar needs to have the knowledge that the dupe (‘dupe’ is used as a term for a person who is ‘lied to’) maintains a different belief from his, that the lying is aiming to change this belief, and, consequently, to change the dupe's behaviour. Three interviews of borderline patients are discussed. It is claimed that borderline patients do not always have access within their own minds to what is definitely an accurate representation of their inner truth, as opposed to the object's representation of the truth. The borderline patient does not have the equipment needed to establish the boundaries of the self. Lying is thus examined as primitive in nature. Two modes of lying are proposed. The common one is lying without taking into account the other's mind. In this type, the patient has learnt to apply an instrumental chain of associations. The other mode aims at establishing the inner truth which does not exist within the self, and can only exist as part of the interaction with the other. The latter may function as a repetitive process directed in the service of the fragmented self reconstruction.

[[RunningHead]]

Trauma, Dora and the Oedipus Complex11

Stephen Gladwell

Stephen Gladwell

Uffculme Clinic, Birmingham, B13 8QD

Stephen Gladwell  

This paper discusses the thesis that traumatic events in childhood (especially sexual abuse) tend to lead to the destruction of the normal oedipal-phase changes because the ordinary developmental fantasies are inhibited. Freud's fascination with the oedipal story is outlined, and the case of ‘Dora’ is used as a well-known example to demonstrate some of the difficulties and pitfalls for therapists working with abused patients.

The ‘seduction theory’ is discussed, and Freud's reluctance to abandon it. Balint's view of the context of abuse within the close confines of the ‘traumatogenic object’ is discussed. Although we now have much more information available to us about the nature and extent of abuse, and a variety of different models which attempt to explain its genesis, it is suggested that the dilemmas that presented to Freud are still important today, perhaps particularly for the male therapist. A plea is made not to re-abuse the patient by ‘accommodating’ to the story of early trauma by labelling it as a state of confusion between fact and fantasy.

responseSet.title: The Body of thought: Psychoanalytic Considerations on the Mind-Body Relationship
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Body of thought: Psychoanalytic Considerations on the Mind-Body Relationship

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter

22 Dyne Road, London, NW6 7XE.

Joan Schachter  

This paper is an introductory review of a complex area of development and pathology. Psychoanalytic thinking about the mind-body relationship originated in Freud's studies of hysteria, in which the patient's body exhibited the unresolved mental conflicts. Subsequent psychoanalytic research into early development focused on the emergence of the psychological self from the early mother-infant relationship with its predominantly bodily experiences. The vicissitudes of the mind-body relationship have to be considered within the context of development of object-relationships and affect regulation.

responseSet.title: Transference N Countertransference Dynamics in the Assessment Process
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference N Countertransference Dynamics in the Assessment Process11

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter

63-65 Lancaster Road, London W11 1QT

Joan Schachter  

In this paper I consider the uses of the transference and countertransference dynamics in the assessment process. Patients referred to an NHS Outpatient Psychotherapy Department are discussed. In the Department the assessor will usually not be the patient's therapist. In this situation I suggest that the transference relationship is not the sole or central focus of interpretation, unless the transference is manifested as a resistance to the exploration of the patient's unconscious conflicts underlying the presenting symptoms and difficulties. However, the transference relationship and countertransference responses alongside the patient's history form the basis of the assessor's understanding and dynamic formulation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcissistic Wounds. Clinical Perspectives. Edited by Judy Cooper & Nilda Maxwell. Whurr Publishers. Pp. 163.

Ronald Doctor

Ronald Doctor

Ronald Doctor  

Though the work of American thinkers, such as Kernberg and Kohut, on the advancement of the understanding of narcissistic disturbances is well known, the contribution of the British school has a comparatively low profile. This book seems to redress the balance, by providing an up-to-date presentation of the work in this area by British psychotherapists and psychoanalysts of different theoretical orientations.

The book as a whole is a convincing picture of the narcissistic patient found in various settings in the National Health Service. Narcissistic patients challenge our therapeutic enthusiasm, often in very disturbing ways — thwarting attempts at reaching them, yet at the same time urgently seeking an object with whom to relate.

The book is divided into three sections: the first, an overview of the important theoretical and clinical literature; the second on narcissism in the life-cycle, containing chapters on infancy, adolescence, couples, childbearing, aging and bereavement; and the third concerned with the clinical aspects of narcissism, with particular reference to perversions and addictions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pregnancy: The Inside Story. By Joan Raphael-Leff. Sheldon Press. £12.99. Pp. 215.

Virginia Grosser

Virginia Grosser

Virginia Grosser  

This is a useful book for any psychoanalytic psychotherapist seeing a patient during her pregnancy — or indeed just before or after pregnancy. It is extremely comprehensive, to the extent of discussing, towards the end of the book, the effects on both patient and therapist of the therapist herself being childless, a parent, or pregnant. I did feel the author was under pressure to cover absolutely every possible psychoanalytic factor and viewpoint — an aspect of her writing particularly apparent in her earlier book, Psychological Processes of Childbearing, but perhaps this is hardly a justifiable complaint!

The author employs a system of dividing pregnant women into three categories, according to their psychological orientation: the Facilitator, whose centre of gravity shifts inwards and who denies ambivalence; the Regulator, whose life-style changes as little as possible, the baby instead having to make adaptations; and the Reciprocator, having a circular as opposed to a linear orientation to the pregnancy, with a capacity to remain aware of ambivalence and internal contradictions. These distinctions provide a very useful framework, lending clarity and continuity to the narrative, and supported by well-chosen excerpts from both individual and group recordings.

The greater accessibility of unconscious material when working with pregnant women, and the way in which this affects the theoretical process are investigated at length, and attention is also given to resistances, such as fantasies of the baby ‘listening in’ to the therapy, and competition around attention or references to the emotional needs of the baby within, and the emotional needs of the woman's baby-self.
